elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a universal weight system (a function on chord diagrams satisfying the $4$-term relation) taking values in the ring of polynomials in infinitely many variables whose particular specializations are weight systems associated with the Lie algebras $\fso(N)$, $\fsp(2M)$, as well as Lie superalgebras $\fosp(N|2M)$.
elements.sent-2411-002: We extend this weight system to permutations and provide an efficient recursion for its computation.
elements.sent-2411-003: The construction for this weight system extends a similar construction for the universal polynomial weight system responsible for the Lie algebras $\fgl(N)$ and superalgebras $\fgl(N|M)$ introduced earlier by the second named author.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Weight systems and Vassiliev knot invariants
elements.sent-2411-004: Below, we use standard notions from the theory of finite order knot invariants; see, e.g.~\cite{Chmutov2012,lando2013graphs .}
elements.sent-2411-005: A {\it chord diagram\/ of order~$m$ is an oriented circle (called the Wilson loop) endowed with~$2m$ pairwise distinct points split into~$m$ disjoint pairs, considered up to orientation-preserving diffeomorphisms of the circle.}
elements.sent-2411-006: These pairs of points are usually depicted as chords.
elements.sent-2411-007: A~{\it weight system\/ is a function $w$ on chord diagrams satisfying the $4$-term relations; see Fig.~\ref{fourtermrelation .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In figures, the outer circle of the chord diagram is always assumed to be oriented counterclockwise.
elements.sent-2411-009: Dashed arcs may contain ends of arbitrary sets of chords, same for all the four terms in the picture.
elements.sent-2411-010: Weight systems play a crucial role in the construction of Vassiliev knot invariants.
elements.sent-2411-011: There is a general construction for a weight system $w_\fg$ associated with an arbitrary Lie algebra~$\fg$ equipped with an ${\rm ad}$-invariant nondegenerate symmetric bilinear form~\cite{kon1993,bar1995vassiliev .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It takes values in the center on the universal enveloping algebra $U\fg$.
elements.sent-2411-013: For the case $\fg=\fgl(N)$, this center is freely generated by the Casimir elements $C_{\fgl(N),1 ,\dots,C_{\fgl(N),N}$, where \begin{equation}\label{eq:glCasimir} C_{\fgl(N),k}=\sum\limits_{i_1,i_2,\dots, i_k=1}^{N}E_{i_1i_2}E_{i_2i_3}\dots E_{i_ki_1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Here $E_{i\,j}\in\fgl(N)$ is the matrix unit having~$1$ on the intersection of the $i$th row and the $j$th column and~$0$ elsewhere, $i,j=1,\dots,N$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, $w_{\fgl(N)}$ takes values in the commutative ring $\C[C_{\fgl(N),1 ,\dots,C_{\fgl(N),N ]$ of polynomials in $N$ independent variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These weight systems are quite strong and have a number of important applications.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the direct computation of this weight system from its definition is rather laborious, since one has to deal with a complicated noncommutative algebra~$U\fgl(N)$.
elements.sent-2411-018: In addition, for a fixed chord diagram $D$ the complexity of computation of~$w_{\fgl(N) (D)$ grows very rapidly with the growth of~$N$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This difficulty was overcome in~\cite{Y1 by presenting a construction for a universal polynomial weight system, denoted by~$w_\fgl$, possessing the following properties (we recall the definition of~$w_\fgl$ below in the next section):.}
elements.sent-2411-020: For a given chord diagram~$D$ with $k$ chords, the polynomial $w_{\fgl (D)$ involves the variables $C_0,\dots,C_k$ only;.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The specialization taking~$w_\fgl$ to $w_{\fgl(N)}$ may involve the Casimir elements $C_{\fgl(N),k}$ defined by~\eqref{eq:glCasimir for $k>N$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: These elements also belong to the center of~$U\fgl(N)$, and they can be expressed as polynomials in $C_{\fgl(N),1 ,\dots,C_{\fgl(N),N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: These polynomial expressions for higher Casimirs can be obtained from the following formula due to Perelomov and Popov, which identifies the ring $ZU\fgl(N)=\C[C_{\fgl(N),1 ,\dots,C_{\fgl(N),N ]$ with the ring of symmetric functions in the indeterminates $x_1,\dots,x_N$, see~\cite{Perelomov-Popov and~\cite[\S60]{Zh : \begin{equation}1-N\,u-\sum_{k=1}^\infty C_{\fgl(N),k}\,u^{k+1}=\prod_{i=1}^N\left(1-\frac{1}{1-x_iu}\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Equating the first $N$ coefficients in these generating series allows one to identify $C_{\fgl(N),1 ,\dots,C_{\fgl(N),N}$ as the generators of the ring of symmetric functions while the remaining terms of this equality can be used to express $C_{\fgl(N),k}$ in terms of these generators for~$k>N$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Existence of~$w_\fgl$ implies that for a fixed chord diagram~$D$ with $k$ chords and $N>k$ the invariant $w_{\fgl(N) (D)$ written as a polynomial in the Casimir elements $C_i=C_{\fgl(N),i}$ involves the generators $C_1,\dots,C_k$ only, and moreover, the coefficients of this polynomial depend on~$N$ in a polynomial way.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This fact is not obvious at all from the definition of~$w_{\fgl(N)}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: The weight system associated with the Lie algebra~$\fsl(N)$ can also be obtained from the same universal one $w_\fgl$ by setting additionally $C_1=0$, which is not surprising, see~\cite{Y1 ,~\cite{KL .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: What is much more surprising is that the same universal weight system~$w_\fgl$ contains also the values of the weight system associated with the Lie superalgebras $\fgl(N|M)$ for all~$N$ and~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-029: A construction of the weight system associated with a metrized Lie superalgebra is parallel to the one associated with a metrized Lie algebra, see~\cite{Chmutov2012,FKV97 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The main result of~\cite{Y2 claims that for the case of the Lie superalgebra~$\fgl(N|M)$ the weight system $w_{\fgl(N|M)}$ can be obtained from $w_\fgl$ by the specialization taking $C_0$ to $N-M$ and $C_k$ to the corresponding Casimir elements.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The Casimir elements of $\fgl(N|M)$ generate the center of~$U\fgl(N|M)$, and relations among them are implied by the following analog of the Perelomov-Popov formula, which identifies the center of $U\fgl(N|M)$ with the ring of supersymmetric functions in the indeterminates $x_1,\dots,x_N,y_1,\dots,y_M$: \begin{equation}1-(N-M)\,u-\sum_{k=1}^\infty C_{\fgl(N|M),k}\,u^{k+1}= \prod_{i=1}^N\frac{1-(1+x_i)\,u}{1-x_i u}. \prod_{j=1}^M\frac{1+y_j\,u}{1-(1-y_j)u}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: The goal of this paper is to find the analogues of the weight system~$w_\fgl$ for other classical series of Lie algebras and superalgebras, and to establish relationship between these universal polynomial invariants.

elements.section-2411-002: Construction of the universal $\fso$ weight system
elements.sent-2411-066: We define in this section a multiplicative weight system $w_\fso$ taking values in the ring of polynomials in the generators $C_0,C_2,C_4,C_6,\dots$ labelled by even nonnegative integers.
elements.sent-2411-067: Similarly to the case of the $w_\fgl$ weight system, we extend the definition of $w_\fso$ to the set of permutations (on any number of permuted elements).
elements.sent-2411-068: This extension is defined by a set of relations that are close to those for the $w_\fgl$ case.
elements.sent-2411-069: The defining relations presented below are motivated by the requirement that for the Lie algebra $\fso(N)$ (with $N$ of any parity) the specialization of $w_\fso$ taking $C_k$ to the corresponding Casimir element is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:wsonsigma} w_{\fso(N)}(s)=\sum_{i_1,\cdots,i_m=1}^N X_{i_1i_{s(1)}}X_{i_2i_{s(2)}}\cdots X_{i_mi_{s(m)}}\in U\fso(N),\end{equation} where $X_{ij}$ are the standard generators of $\fso(N)$, see Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-070: We show in the next section that the weight systems associated with the Lie algebras $\fsp(2M)$ as well as the Lie superalgebras $\fosp(N|2M)$ are also specializations of the universal weight system $w_\fso$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Before formulating defining relations for $w_\fso$, we introduce some notation used in these relations.
elements.sent-2411-072: The invariant $w_\fso$ constructed below possesses an additional symmetry that does not hold for the $\fgl$ case:.
elements.sent-2411-073: In this case the values $w_\fso(s)$ and $w_\fso(s')$ differ by the sign factor $(-1)^r$ where $r$ is the length of the cycle.
elements.sent-2411-074: This symmetry leads to the following convention.
elements.sent-2411-075: Along with the digraphs of permutations we consider more general graphs which we call extended permutation graphs.
elements.sent-2411-076: An extended permutation graph is a graph with the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-077: For two half-edges adjacent to every vertex one of them is a tail and the other is a head.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, we allow for the edges of the graph to have two heads, or two tails, or a head and a tail.
elements.sent-2411-079: We extend $w_\fso$ to graphs of this kind by the following additional relation: a change of the tail and the head for the half-edges adjacent to any vertex results in the multiplication of the invariant by $-1$: \begin{equation}w_\fso\left(~ \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8] \draw (0.9,0) node[v] (V) {}; \draw [->] (0,0) -- (1.8,0); \draw[->,blue] (1.8,1) .. controls +(-0.6,-0.5) .. elements.sent-2411-082: The universal polynomial invariant $w_\fso$ is the function on the set of permutations of any number of elements (or, equivalently, on the set permutation graphs) taking values in the ring of polynomials in the generators $C_0,C_2,C_4,\dots$ and defined by the following set of relations (axioms).

elements.sent-2411-084: A change of orientation of any cycle of length~$r$ in the graph results in multiplication of the value of the invariant~$w_\fso$ by~$(-1)^r$.

elements.sent-2411-094: The proof of Theorem~\ref{th:so goes as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Using defining relations any graph can be reduced to a product of independent cycles of both even and odd length.
elements.sent-2411-096: Next we observe that any cycle is conjugate to its inverse in the permutation group.
elements.sent-2411-097: It follows that the difference between a cycle and its inverse can be expressed modulo the relations in terms of permutations on smaller number of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-098: If the length of the cycle is even this relation says nothing about the value of $w_\fso$ on this cycle.
elements.sent-2411-099: But in the case when the length of the cycle is odd we obtain that this cycle taken twice is expressed in terms of permutations with smaller number of elements.
elements.sent-2411-100: Applying this procedure repeatedly we finally express any permutation (permutation graph) as a polynomial (a linear combination of monomials) in the standard cycles of even length~$2k$ for which the value of $w_\fso$ is denoted by~$C_{2k}$.
elements.sent-2411-101: Next we need to show that the obtained polynomial expression for a given permutation in terms of generators is unique, that is, independent of the order in which we applied the recurrence relations to obtain this expression.
elements.sent-2411-102: For that we consider the weight systems associated with the classical Lie algebra $\fso(N)$, and define the extension of the corresponding weight system $w_{\fso(N)}$ to the set of permutations using~\eqref{eq:wsonsigma .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Then, we prove in the next section that this invariant satisfies defining relations of~$w_\fso$ weight system, see Theorem~\ref{th:soNspN .}
elements.sent-2411-104: We conclude the proof of Theorem~\ref{th:so by the arguments similar to those for the case of the $w_\fgl$ weight system.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Using recurrence relations, we can express the value of $w_{\fso(N)}$ on a given permutation as a polynomial in the Casimir elements corresponding to cyclic permutations of even length.
elements.sent-2411-106: The Casimir elements belong to the center of $U\fso(N)$ which implies that $w_{\fso(N) (s)$ also belongs to the center.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Moreover, the Casimir elements $C_{\fso(N),2 ,C_{\fso(N),4 ,\dots,C_{\fso(N),[N/2]}$ are algebraically independent.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: It follows that for a fixed permutation and sufficiently large~$N$ the polynomial expression for this permutation in terms of Casimirs is uniquely defined (for the specialization $C_0=N$).
elements.sent-2411-109: The coefficients of this polynomial depend on~$N$ in a polynomial way and these polynomials in~$N$ are uniquely reconstructed from a finite collection of (sufficiently large) values of~$N$.
elements.sent-2411-110: The multiplicativity of $w_\fso$ holds because it obviously holds for $w_{\fso(N)}$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The $4$-term relation also follows from the fact that it holds in the case of the $\fso(N)$ weight system (as for a weight system associated with a metrized Lie algebra).
elements.sent-2411-112: This completes the proof of Theorem~\ref{th:so .}
elements.sent-2411-113: If $N$ is odd, the center of $U\fso(N)$ is generated by Casimirs.
elements.sent-2411-114: If $N=2N_1$ is even, then the center of $U\fso(2N_1)$ along with the Casimir elements contains also an extra generator which is not expressed in terms of the Casimirs, see\cite{nwachuku1979 .}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Proof of cyclic invariance of~$w_\fgl$ and ~$w_\fso$
elements.sent-2411-122: The values of the weight systems $w_{\fgl}$ and $w_{\fso}$ depend on the cyclic order on the set of permuted elements only.
elements.sent-2411-123: In other words, a conjugation of a permutation $s\in S(m)$ by the cyclic permutation $1\mapsto 2\mapsto\dots\mapsto m\mapsto 1$ preserves the values of both weight systems.
elements.sent-2411-124: By induction on the number of permuted elements, let $s\in S(m)$ and $s^{cyc}\in S(m)$ be the conjugation of $s$ by the cyclic permutation $1\mapsto 2\mapsto\dots\mapsto m\mapsto 1$.
elements.sent-2411-125: Assume the cyclic invariance of $w_\fgl$ and $w_\fso$ on all permutations with less than $m$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-126: Since the right side of the recursion of $w_\fgl$ and $w_\fso$ is the difference of switching two neighbouring legs, the left side contains only the weight systems on permutations with less elements.. By induction, \begin{equation}w_\fg(s)-w_\fg(s^{cyc})=w_\fg(s_{i,i+1})-w_\fg(s_{i,i+1}^{cyc}), 1<i<m-1,\fg=\fgl,\fso.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-131: The extension to the set of permutations for the weight systems associated with the Lie algebras $\fso(N)$ and $\fsp(2M)$, and Lie superalgebra $\fosp(N|2M)$ take values in the center of the corresponding universal enveloping algebras and obey the defining relations of the universal $w_\fso$ weight system, with the specialization of $C_k$ to the standard Casimir elements for $k\ge1$, and with $C_0=N$ for the Lie algebra $\fso(N)$, $C_0=-2M$ for the Lie algebra $\fsp(2M)$, and $C_0=N-2M$ for the Lie superalgebra~$\fosp(N|2M)$.

elements.sent-2411-133: By that reason, the statement of Theorem concerning the Lie algebras $\fso(N)$ and $\fsp(2M)$ follows from the one for the Lie superalgebra $\fosp(N|2M)$.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, we consider the cases of the Lie algebras $\fso(N)$ and $\fsp(2M)$ independently because the arguments admit some simplifications in these cases.

elements.sent-2411-210: As a corollary, we obtain a relation expressing odd Casimirs in terms of even ones, and this relation is universal in the sense that it does not depend on the dimensions~$N$ and~$2M$.
elements.sent-2411-211: In all studied cases $\fg=\fso(N)$, $\fsp(2M)$ or $\fosp(N|2M)$ the corresponding universal enveloping algebra $U\fg$ admits the direct sum decomposition \begin{equation}U\fg=(n_-\;U\fg+U\fg\; n_+)\oplus Uh,\end{equation} where $\mathfrak{n _-$, $\mathfrak{h}$ and $\mathfrak{n _+$ are the subalgebras of~$\fg$ spanned by $X_{ij}$ with $i>j$, $i=j$, and $i<j$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: The Harish-Chandra homomorphism can be defined as the projection to the second summand of this decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-213: Its restriction to the center $ZU\fg$ is injective which allows one to identify this center with certain subalgebra in the algebra of polynomials in the generators $X_{i,i}$.
elements.sent-2411-214: The Perelomov-Popov formula describes the Casimir elements through this identification.
elements.sent-2411-215: It expresses the Casimir elements as (super)symmetric polynomials in the squares of the properly shifted generators $X_{i,i}$.
elements.sent-2411-216: More explicitly, let us collect Casimir elements into the following generating series in (the inverse powers of) the variable $u$ \begin{equation}F(u)=1-\Bigl(\frac{u-\frac{C_0-1}{2}}{u-\frac{C_0-2}{2}}\Bigr)\;\sum_{m=0}^\infty C_m u^{-m-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-217: Then the analog of Perelomov-Popov formula for this case derived in~\cite{NR can be represented in the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:PP} F(u)=\frac{P\bigl(u-\frac{C_0}{2}\bigr)}{P\bigl(u-\frac{C_0-2}{2}\bigr)},\end{equation} where $P$ is a rational function given by an explicit formula depending on the chosen algebra $\fg$.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Then we have \begin{equation}P(u)= \phantom{u\,}\prod_{i=1}^{\frac{N}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2), N is even, [1ex] u\,\prod_{i=1}^{\frac{N-1}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2), N is odd. \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-039: \begin{equation}P(u)= \phantom{u\,}\prod_{i=1}^{\frac{N}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2), N is even, [1ex] u\,\prod_{i=1}^{\frac{N-1}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2), N is odd. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-222: Then we have \begin{equation}P(u)= \phantom{u\,}\prod_{i=1}^{M+\frac{N}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2)^{\sigma_i}, N is even, [1ex] u\,\prod_{i=1}^{M+\frac{N-1}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2)^{\sigma_i}, N is odd. \end{equation} In all the cases the function $P(u)$ is either even or odd so that $P(u)/P(-u)=\pm 1$.
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation}P(u)= \phantom{u\,}\prod_{i=1}^{M+\frac{N}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2)^{\sigma_i}, N is even, [1ex] u\,\prod_{i=1}^{M+\frac{N-1}{2}}(u^2-x_i^2)^{\sigma_i}, N is odd. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-224: There are universal polynomial expressions independent of~$N$ and $2M$ for odd Casimir elements of the Lie superalgebra $\fosp(N|2M)$ in terms of even ones.
elements.sent-2411-225: More explicitly, these relations can be expressed as the following equality for the generating series for Casimirs \begin{equation}F(u)F(C_0-1-u)=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-226: The first few of these relations are present in Remark~\ref{rem:Casimirs .}
